---
title: "Optional Advanced: Fitting ODE Models"
format:
  revealjs:
    toc: false
    smaller: true
    scrollable: true
---






## Why this is optional

This deck parks the old ODE-fitting extension from the combined ODE/optimization
material. It is useful for learners who want to see how simulation code can be
wrapped in an objective function, but it is not part of the Day 3 ODE core.

The point here is the programming pattern, not a recommended inference workflow.

## Data

The observed epidemic curve is local to this repository.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
observed_sir <- read.csv(file.path("..", "data", "SIR-observed.csv"))
head(observed_sir)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


| day| total_cases|
|---:|-----------:|
|   1|           1|
|   2|           5|
|   3|          22|
|   4|          44|
|   5|         109|
|   6|         271|
:::
:::

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(
  observed_sir$day,
  observed_sir$total_cases,
  pch = 16,
  col = "gray30",
  xlab = "Day",
  ylab = "Observed infected",
  main = "Observed epidemic curve"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](advanced-ODE-parameter-fitting_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::




## Reuse the SIR model




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sir_ode <- function(t, state, parameters) {
  S <- state["S"]
  I <- state["I"]
  R <- state["R"]

  beta <- parameters["beta"]
  gamma <- parameters["gamma"]

  dS <- -beta * S * I
  dI <- beta * S * I - gamma * I
  dR <- gamma * I

  list(c(S = dS, I = dI, R = dR))
}
```
:::




## Write a simulation helper




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
simulate_sir <- function(beta, gamma, times) {
  deSolve::ode(
    y = c(S = 999, I = 1, R = 0),
    times = times,
    func = sir_ode,
    parms = c(beta = beta, gamma = gamma)
  )
}

example_simulation <- simulate_sir(
  beta = 0.001,
  gamma = 0.05,
  times = observed_sir$day
)

head(example_simulation)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


| time|        S|          I|         R|
|----:|--------:|----------:|---------:|
|    1| 999.0000|   1.000000| 0.0000000|
|    2| 997.3353|   2.581321| 0.0833883|
|    3| 993.0569|   6.644762| 0.2983410|
|    4| 982.1663|  16.983963| 0.8497058|
|    5| 955.1094|  42.644174| 2.2464489|
|    6| 891.7527| 102.569003| 5.6783062|
:::
:::




## Compare one simulation to the data




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(
  observed_sir$day,
  observed_sir$total_cases,
  pch = 16,
  col = "gray30",
  xlab = "Day",
  ylab = "Infected",
  main = "Observed and simulated infected counts"
)
lines(example_simulation[, "time"], example_simulation[, "I"], col = "darkorange2", lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topright",
  legend = c("Observed", "Simulated"),
  col = c("gray30", "darkorange2"),
  pch = c(16, NA),
  lty = c(NA, 1),
  lwd = c(NA, 2),
  bty = "n"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](advanced-ODE-parameter-fitting_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::




## Objective function

We need one number that says how badly a parameter pair fits.

Here we use the sum of squared residuals between observed infected counts and
the simulated infected compartment:

$$
SSR = \sum_i \left(I_i - \hat{I}_i(\beta, \gamma)\right)^2
$$




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sir_ssr <- function(par) {
  beta <- par[1]
  gamma <- par[2]

  if (beta <= 0 || gamma <= 0) {
    return(1e12)
  }

  simulated <- tryCatch(
    simulate_sir(
      beta = beta,
      gamma = gamma,
      times = observed_sir$day
    ),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )

  if (is.null(simulated)) {
    return(1e12)
  }

  predicted_infected <- simulated[, "I"]

  if (any(!is.finite(predicted_infected))) {
    return(1e12)
  }

  fit <- sum((observed_sir$total_cases - predicted_infected)^2)

  if (!is.finite(fit)) {
    return(1e12)
  }

  fit
}
```
:::




## Fit the parameters




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sir_fit <- optim(
  par = c(beta = 0.001, gamma = 0.05),
  fn = sir_ssr,
  method = "L-BFGS-B",
  lower = c(beta = 0.000001, gamma = 0.000001),
  upper = c(beta = 0.01, gamma = 1)
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
DLSODA-  At T (=R1), too much accuracy requested

In above message, R1 = 2, R2 = nan
```

:::




## Plot the fitted trajectory




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fitted_simulation <- simulate_sir(
  beta = sir_fit$par["beta"],
  gamma = sir_fit$par["gamma"],
  times = observed_sir$day
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
DLSODA-  At T (=R1), too much accuracy requested  
      for precision of machine..  See TOLSF (=R2) 
In above message, R1 = 2, R2 = nan
 
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in lsoda(y, times, func, parms, ...): Excessive precision requested.
scale up `rtol' and `atol' e.g by the factor 10
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in lsoda(y, times, func, parms, ...): Returning early. Results are
accurate, as far as they go
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(
  observed_sir$day,
  observed_sir$total_cases,
  pch = 16,
  col = "gray30",
  xlab = "Day",
  ylab = "Infected",
  main = "Observed and fitted SIR trajectory"
)
lines(fitted_simulation[, "time"], fitted_simulation[, "I"], col = "darkorange2", lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topright",
  legend = c("Observed", "Fitted SIR"),
  col = c("gray30", "darkorange2"),
  pch = c(16, NA),
  lty = c(NA, 1),
  lwd = c(NA, 2),
  bty = "n"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](advanced-ODE-parameter-fitting_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::




## Practice checkpoint

**Prompt**

Change the starting values in `optim()` to `beta = 0.005` and `gamma = 0.50`.
Do the fitted parameters return to approximately the same values?

**Hint**

Only change the `par = ...` line. Keep the same bounds.

**Solution outline**

Run the same call with:

```r
par = c(beta = 0.005, gamma = 0.50)
```

For this small example, the bounds and data are informative enough that the
result should be very similar. For more complex ODE systems, starting values can
matter a lot.

## Take-home cautions

- Real case data are noisy, delayed, underreported, and often only partly
  observed.
- The observation model matters as much as the ODE model.
- Parameter uncertainty from this quick example is not reliable.
- Use this deck to learn how to connect simulation code to a fitting workflow,
  then reach for a more serious modeling framework for real inference.

<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
